`context` has a start and end position, so it must know the length of everything. Unfortunately, it does not provide a start position for each token, it only does that for the production rule as a whole. (That is, what I really want is `context.token_start_position[token_index]`.) As a work-around, I need to calculate the offset from the production rule start to the token, so I can use the start position of the rule, and apply `parglare.pos_to_line_col` to get a human-readable terminal position.

I wrote this code, since in my opinion, line (and column) information is required in almost all non-trivial programs that use a parser. Parglare doesn't provide a standard function for it, so I think almost every Parglare user will have to dig through the API in the manual like I did, figuring out how to get useful position information. The example code shows how to do it in a common use case (ie parsing plain text).

While for you, parsing is the one and only job, for me, parsing is just the simple first step (takes a day or two if the language design is finished, and if it fits nicely in the parser assumptions).
Right behind it is static semantics checking and/or interpretation of the input, and error detection and reporting about the input is a dominant part there (takes several months at least rather than 2 days). In my experience, you always need line/column position from the input (with ascii-ish text being the dominant stuff being parsed, and the need to be able to fallback to dumping error messages onto `stderr`. (That is, `incorrect type at line 32, column 54` is so much nicer than `incorrect type in the type definition starting at offset 33536`.)

I agree you need length of a token in my code, but I don't know how else to get a `(line, column)` position of an arbitrary terminal in a production rule. As a secondary consideration, in case there is no length of a token, my guess is that a `(line, column)` notion won't make sense either, ie, you don't need this code in that case.

I didn't look at building a parse tree, since my actions immediately abstract to an AST. I don't want a tree with all irrelevant stuff still in there, since I will have to crawl over it with manually written code rather than with your automagically generated callbacks. (That is, a parse tree is more work.)

Because there is an issue with the way broadcast operator tests are written in ONNX. 

For example, if we try to broadcast (5,) dim array on (3,4,5) dim array then mxnet's forward pass will fail because the mxnet's interface expects the same batch size on the two arrays, i.e. (1,5) and (1,3,4,5)

So 
```
x = mx.nd.array(np.random.rand(3,4,5))
y = mx.nd.array(np.random.rand(5,))
mx.nd.broadcast_add(x,y)
```
will pass, but the following will fail
```
xvar = mx.sym.var('x')
yvar = mx.sym.var('y')
bcast_add = mx.sym.broadcast_add(xvar, yvar)
```

There are broadcast operators in the various models that are being tested and they work fine, as the data in such models come with a valid batch_size.

This is a **caveat**. The current implementation allows to:
* set a pin in a global variable (i.e. `requests` library to set global settings)
* when an instance is created from a class that has a `Pin` attached, the pin reference is copied to the instance, meaning they both point to the same object
* because the `config` object is a mutable value, it means that changing the configuration for an "instance" pin, changes also the global configuration for all other pins
* this results in an error

All of this is done to keep the API simple, while reusing the `Pin` class. Another approach is to continue to expose and suggest the `Pin` API, that I would rather to hide / remove from the public facing API.

So what's happening here is somewhat subtle. The string that's sent across the wire, I believe, is encoded as UTF8 regardless of the encoding the language uses. However, the API needs to calculate offsets into the string for the entities/etc it detects, which will differ depending on the codec. For example, given the string `1😶2`, in UTF8, the index for `2` is 5. In UTF16, it's 3. In UTF32, it's 2. This is because the index into the string is an index into an array of entries of the smallest 'character' size - 8-bits for UTF8, 16-bits for UTF16, 32-bits for UTF32. So double-byte characters will take up two slots in a UTF8-encoded string, and one slot in a UTF16-encoded string; and triple-byte characters will take up three slots in a UTF8-encoded string, and two slots in a UTF16-encoded string; etc.

So the API needs to know what encoding to calculate the offsets for.

python2 `string` objects are basically byte arrays that are encoded in ascii by default. This barfs on unicode:

```python
>>> a = u'1😶2'
>>> str(a)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\U0001f636' in position 1: ordinal not in range(128)
```

You can explicitly specify the encoding for unicode characters, for example to UTF8:

```python
>>> a.encode('utf8')
'1\xf0\x9f\x98\xb62'
>>> a.encode('utf8')[5]
'2'
```
in which case you would pass UTF8 to the API.

But if you're using `unicode` objects (ie not explicitly using a particular encoding), you get either UTF32 or UTF16:

```python
>>> sys.maxunicode
1114111
>>> a[2]
u'2'
```
depending on what your python interpreter is compiled as.

get_header_from_flask():

get_trace_id():
     checkers = [get_header_from_django, get_header_from_flask]

     for checker in checkers:
         header = checker()

    return header

So, a possible example would be:

* Thread 1 does something to an object that locks that object but later (before releasing the object lock) requires a lock on the registry
* Thread 2 then requests the same object from the registry before the other thread has a lock on the registry, thus locking it first.
* Thread 1 is then waiting to get the registry lock from Thread 2
* But, Thread 2 (because of the request for the registry ID from the object) is waiting on for Thread 1 to release it's lock on the object.
* and you're stuck...

The `all()` syntax here is a bit excessive for only checking one key at a time. It's useful when there are a bunch of keys that need to be checked, but the `in` operator is fine for a single key.

```
required_key_list_login = ['unique_id']
required_keys_present_login = all((x in login for x in required_key_list_login))
```
is equivalent to 
```
required_keys_present_login = 'unique_id' in required_key_list_login
```

You can even take it a step further and replace the variable below with just the `in` statement, since it's only used once.
